agent_outputs.raw: The promotion model proposal makes a fairness claim that is not supported by the provided evidence. The evaluation reports overall macro-F1 on historical promotion decisions — this is a predictive accuracy metric, not a fairness metric. Fairness requires specifying a criterion (equal opportunity, calibration, demographic parity) and measuring it at the subgroup level. The evaluation packet shows only an aggregate confusion matrix with no gender or intersectional breakdowns. Additionally, training on historical manager recommendations that were approved or rejected by a committee is training on a process that may itself have been biased. A model that more accurately predicts historically biased decisions is not fairer by any standard definition. Removing names and photos from the feature set is a positive step but does not establish fairness — proxy features (name-correlated features, historical compensation) can still encode gender information. The fairness claim is unsupported and requires a specified criterion with subgroup analysis.

The evidence does not support the fairness claim. Three issues: First, macro-F1 improved overall but this says nothing about whether the model treats gender groups more equally — a model can improve overall F1 while increasing disparate impact. Second, no fairness definition is specified and no gender-stratified metrics are reported. Without these, the claim 'fairer across gender groups' has no measurable basis. Third, the training labels are historical manager recommendations — if these recommendations reflect prior gender bias, the model trained to replicate them replicates the bias more systematically. Removing explicit identity features is insufficient to guarantee fairness when proxy features exist.

The proposal's fairness claim is not supported by the evaluation. Macro-F1 is an accuracy metric, not a fairness metric. The evaluation does not specify which fairness criterion is being claimed, does not show gender-stratified error rates, and does not audit the training labels for gender disparity. Historical promotion decisions — especially those involving manager recommendations followed by committee review — are not a normative fairness target. The model should be evaluated on specified fairness criteria with subgroup analysis before any fairness claim is made. The temporal holdout and name removal are positive methodological practices but do not constitute a fairness evaluation.

synthesis: All three agents identify the same three issues and conclude critique wins. Macro-F1 is not a fairness metric (unanimous), no subgroup analysis or specified criterion exists (unanimous), and historical labels may embed bias (unanimous). The positive features (name removal, temporal split) are correctly identified as valid practices that do not support the fairness claim.

etd_constraint.condition: Specify fairness criterion; compute gender-stratified false positive and false negative rates on test split; audit historical training labels for gender disparity controlling for observable qualifications
etd_constraint.supports_critique_if: Model shows higher false negative rate for women than men, or historical labels show gender disparity after controlling for job level and performance metrics
etd_constraint.supports_defense_if: Under a pre-specified fairness criterion, model shows equal or improved subgroup error rates relative to the historical manual process
etd_constraint.ambiguous_if: Model improves equal opportunity but worsens demographic parity (or vice versa), leaving criterion choice to the company

all_issues_raised: Macro-F1 is not a fairness metric (all three agents)
No subgroup analysis or specified fairness criterion (all three agents)
Historical promotion labels may embed gender bias (all three agents)
